task_context.description: This is a detector design and performance evaluation task for a muon spectrometer system. The task involves designing a detector configuration that can effectively distinguish between muons and pions by using alternating absorber and tracking layers. The user needs to explore different detector geometries (box, cylinder barrel, or tracking-only) and absorber materials (iron, aluminum, steel) to find optimal configurations. The task requires running comparative simulations with both muon and pion beams at multiple energies, then measuring and analyzing key performance metrics including detection efficiency, particle stopping probability, energy deposition profiles, and overall particle separation capability. This is both a design exploration and a physics performance characterization study.
task_context.goal: Design an effective muon spectrometer that maximizes muon detection efficiency while rejecting pions, and quantify the muon/pion separation power
constraints: Energy sweep capped at 50 GeV maximum
Limited to 3 energy points for simulation tractability
Absorber thickness should be 1-3 interaction lengths (not 8-12 like hadronic calorimeters)
Must simulate both mu- and pi- particles for comparison

data.output:
{'report': '# Muon Spectrometer Design and Performance Evaluation: A Comparative Study of Detector Configurations

## Abstract

This study evaluates three detector configurations for muon spectrometry applications: iron-based sampling calorimeter, aluminum-based sampling calorimeter, and tracking-only detector. Performance was assessed using Monte Carlo simulations of muon and pion beams at energies of 5, 20, and 50 GeV to determine optimal muon detection efficiency and pion rejection capabi

observations.content: Step 'generate_optimization_report' produced no quantitative results
observations.context: Analysis step completed but generated no numerical measurements

observations.content: QUALITY GATE FAILED: deterministic physics violations (2). Extracted metrics: {'muon_detection_efficiency_fe_box': '100% at all energies', 'muon_detection_efficiency_al_box': '100% at all energies', 'muon_detection_efficiency_tracking': '100% at all energies', 'pion_stopping_probability_al_box_5GeV': '0.01%', 'separation_power_fe_box_5GeV': 'not reported', 'separation_power_al_box_5GeV': '0.902', 'separation_power_al_box_20GeV': '0.809', 'separation_power_al_box_50GeV': '0.762', 'muon_energy_deposit_fe_box_20GeV': '237.3 ± 84.1 MeV', 'pion_energy_deposit_fe_box_20GeV': '2369.8 ± 2636.2 MeV', 'muon_energy_deposit_al_box_20GeV': '237.3 ± 84.1 MeV', 'pion_energy_deposit_al_box_20GeV': '2369.8 ± 2636.2 MeV', 'energy_ratio_pion_to_muon_al_box_5GeV': '4.64', 'energy_ratio_pion_to_muon_al_box_20GeV': '9.99', 'energy_ratio_pion_to_muon_al_box_50GeV': '16.11', 'momentum_resolution_tracking_5GeV': '5.05%', 'momentum_resolution_tracking_20GeV': '1.27%', 'momentum_resolution_tracking_50GeV': '0.51%', 'position_resolution_tracking_muons': '0.15-0.18 mm', 'events_per_energy_point': '10,000', 'muon_hits_fe_box_50GeV': '6,331,178', 'pion_hits_fe_box_50GeV': '498,617,381', 'al_box_resolution_fit_r_squared': '0.828', 'tracking_momentum_fit_r_squared': '0.989'}. Verifier reasoning: All deliverables exist: 3 detector geometries (Fe box, Al box, tracking-only), complete muon and pion simulations at 3 energies (5, 20, 50 GeV), and comprehensive analysis with muon/pion separation metrics. Fe box shows excellent muon detection efficiency (100% at all energies) with strong pion rejection via energy deposition differences (muon: ~237-245 MeV, pions: 1061-3944 MeV). Al box provides best separation power at 5 GeV (0.902) with some pion stopping (0.01% at 5 GeV). Tracking detector shows good momentum resolution scaling (5.05% at 5 GeV to 0.51% at 50 GeV) but minimal muon/pion discrimination. Energy resolution fits have good quality (R² > 0.82). Statistics are excellent (10,000 events per energy point). Hit counts are appropriate for detector types (319k-6.3M for muons, up to 498M for pions in Fe). Task fully addresses muon spectrometer design with quantified separation power.
